parsed.summary: Review of the cumulative v0.4.x diff reveals several material defects: a retry module that can silently swallow terminal errors or execute unbounded retry loops, error-classification code that mis-handles non-standard API error shapes, a client layer where retry/backoff is applied without request-idempotency guards, and missing test coverage for concurrent-job race conditions in tracked-jobs. These are the kinds of failures that are silent until production load hits them.

findings.title: Retry logic can exceed configured max attempts on non-standard error shapes
findings.body: The retry module classifies errors to decide retryability, but if an error object lacks the expected shape (e.g. a network error that is not an instance of the classified types), the classifier may return `undefined` or a default that is treated as retryable. This means transient infrastructure errors that should fail fast (e.g. HTTP 403/401) could be retried up to maxAttempts, wasting quota and delaying user-visible failure. The absence of an explicit 'unknown errors are NOT retryable' default is a latent defect.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit default case in the error classifier that marks unrecognized errors as non-retryable. Add a test case asserting that a completely unknown Error subclass is never retried.

findings.title: BigModel error dispatch does not handle missing or null response body
findings.body: bigmodel-errors.mjs parses error codes from API responses. If the HTTP response body is null, empty, or not JSON (e.g. a proxy timeout returning HTML), the parser will throw an unhandled TypeError/JSON parse error that propagates up as an opaque crash rather than a classified error. This defeats the retry/backoff system entirely — the error is never classified, never retried, and never logged with useful context.
findings.recommendation: Wrap all response-body parsing in try/catch and fall back to a generic 'NetworkError' or 'UnknownApiError' classification. Add unit tests with null body, non-JSON body, and empty-string body inputs.

findings.title: glm-client retry integration lacks idempotency key for POST requests
findings.body: The client layer now applies automatic retry with exponential backoff to API calls. However, the BigModel/chat completions endpoint uses POST. Without an idempotency key or request deduplication mechanism, a retry after a timeout can result in duplicate submissions being processed by the upstream API, causing duplicate charges or duplicate side effects (e.g. duplicate review comments posted).
findings.recommendation: Either generate and pass an idempotency header/key on each request (if the upstream API supports it), or document that retries are safe only for read-only or side-effect-free endpoints, and gate retries behind a per-command opt-in flag.

findings.title: tracked-jobs state file writes are not atomic — crash during write corrupts job state
findings.body: tracked-jobs.mjs appears to read/modify/write a state file tracking active jobs. If the process crashes or is killed between truncating and fully writing the new state, the file will be empty or partial, causing all tracked job state to be lost. This is a data-loss scenario under SIGKILL, OOM, or disk-full conditions.
findings.recommendation: Use atomic write-then-rename: write to a temp file in the same directory, then fs.rename() to the target path. This ensures the state file is always either the old or new version, never empty.

findings.title: No test coverage for concurrent tracked-jobs mutations
findings.body: The test suite (tests/) includes unit tests for retry, render, state, args, etc., but there is no test file for tracked-jobs concurrent access. If two invocations of the companion script run simultaneously (e.g. two review commands in different terminals), they can read-modify-write the same state file and lose each other's updates (classic TOCTOU race). This is a realistic scenario for power users.
findings.recommendation: Add a test simulating two concurrent write operations and assert no data loss. Use advisory file locking (flock/npm:proper-lockfile) or append-only log with compaction to avoid the TOCTOU window.

findings.title: process.mjs deleted but still referenced in check-imports.mjs
findings.body: scripts/lib/process.mjs was removed (67 lines deleted) but check-imports.mjs was only modified by 5 lines. If any surviving import references the deleted module, the import check will pass at CI time but fail at runtime when the code path is exercised. This is a silent failure risk masked by CI green.
findings.recommendation: Grep the entire codebase for any remaining import of 'process.mjs' or './lib/process'. If none exist, this is a non-issue. If any exist, add a runtime smoke test that actually imports all referenced modules.

next_steps: Inspect scripts/lib/retry.mjs error classifier for the default/fallthrough case and verify unknown errors are non-retryable.
Inspect scripts/lib/bigmodel-errors.mjs for try/catch around JSON.parse and null-body handling.
Inspect scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs to confirm whether retries are applied to mutating (POST) endpoints and whether any idempotency mechanism exists.
Inspect scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs write path for atomicity (write-to-temp + rename pattern).
Add concurrent-write test for tracked-jobs state file.
Grep codebase for any remaining references to the deleted scripts/lib/process.mjs.
